titleStmt.title: Canto 2: ūralaruraittakātai
The Canto in which Gossip in the City is Reported (75 lines)

summary.p: 2.1-9 Mātavi's mother Cittirāpati, upset about the gossip that has arisen as a result of Maṇimēkalai's and her absence from the Indra Festival, summons Mātavi's confidante Vayantamālai to deliver a message to her.
2.10-17 Vayantamālai finds Mātavi and Maṇimēkalai in the pavillion. They are weakened by asceticism. She asks what has happened.
2.18-32 Vayantamālai lists Mātavi's attainments as a dancer, to remind her of her true calling.
2.33-36 Vayantamālai tells Mātavi that to avoid shaming gossip she must give up her asceticism.
2.37-41 Mātavi replies that since losing her lover Kōvalaṉ she has renounced shame.
2.42-48 Mātavi lists the options open to widows: spontaneous death, the pyre, or penance.
2.49-55b Mātavi cites Kaṇṇaki as a paragon of female virtue.
2.55c -57 Mātavi states her wish that Maṇimēkalai, who is Kaṇṇaki's daughter (by marriage), renounce the courtesan life and become a nun.
2.58-69 Mātavi recounts how, after the death of Kōvalaṉ, she went to a Buddhist teacher, Aṟavaṇa for instruction.

2.70-75 Mātavi concludes her speech to Vayantamālai with a request to report her decision to Cittirāpati and the other women. Having failed to convince Mātavi, Vayantamālai returns in despair to Cittirāpati.

div.p: The second song, in which gossip in the city is reported.

lg.l: On the auspicious days during which the Island Propitation [Festival] was performed—
put on by the protective deity (Campu) for the king of the gods (Indra)
on the great big island high with/famous for Jaumoon-plum trees (Camputtīvu),
Mātavi and Maṇimēkalai did not come.
As for Cittirāpati, she suffered affliction and wept.
Unnabating grief overwhelmed [her] again and again.
Red-streaks spread across her long eyes.
She called Vayantamālai, her daughter's confidante, to come,
and said, 'Tell [my daughter Mātavi] about the gossip [concerning hers and Maṇimēkalai's absence from the festival] in the great fertile city!'
she became sick at heart on seeing the faded body
of that woman with choice jewels and a dancer's grace,
because Mātavi had become a wreck, faded in body, dispirited,
[She] said [to her], 'O you who closely resemble Śrī! Listen to what has happened!
Is there any [emnity] towards you that has arisen in the city?
O gold-bangled lady, you who have studied and become expert in the branches of knowledge [which include]:
playing the tuneful lute and singing melodies;
the Taṇṇumai drum instrument and the low, sweet flute;
the art of [playing] ball and techniques in the art of cooking;
coloured powders and swimming in pure water;
[the art of] the bedroom and seasonal practices;
types of figurative language and the science of staying hidden;
techniques of the painting brush, and selecting and weaving blossoms [into garlands];
astrology and mastery of [all] the arts;
the contents of treatises on rhetoric/commentaries and of the refined treatises on painting
which are well assigned to dancing girls!
Many crowds of people in the vast, ancient city—except for the decent ones—are repeating the embarassing truth:
that [your] practice of good penance is a shameful thing.
This is undesirable—a shameful thing indeed.' Thus spoke [Vayantamālai].
'When I heard about the cruel tragedy which [my] lover [Kōvalaṉ] suffered,
I remained [alive]—with a life that would not depart—
[but] I lost [my] reputation in the ancient city of gold banners/ [my] reputation as a golden creeper (i.e. as a successful woman) in the ancient city—
o lady with good bangles!—and I renounced [my] shame.
Chaste women in this world of wide waters
—if the men [they] love pass away [before them]—
kindle a raging fire [of grief in their hearts],
breathe hard like the nozzle-hole of the blowing smith's forge,
they enter [instead] the blazing fire, as if into a pond of good (i.e. deep) water.
If they do not enter the blazing fire,

they ravage [their] bodies by doing penance for the sake of a life lived together with [their] lovers [in the next birth]/ for the sake of the life [they] lived together with [their] lovers [in this birth].
But our lady of choice jewels (Kaṇṇaki) was not a woman of that kind.
unable to bear the bitter tragedy suffered by [her] husband/for the sake of [her] husband,
[she undid her hair] so that [her] thick fragrant locks streamed down to the base of her spine,
forcibly twisted off [her] shining, young, beautiful breast bathed in tears,
and fed the great city of the (Pāṇṭiyaṉ) King to the blazing conflagration.
[shall suffer] nothing but the practice of arduous penance.
She shall not suffer the sinful profession with its less than ideal acts (i.e. the courtesan profession) at all.
But listen to me further, o [lady of] choice jewels!
I entered the abode of these ascetics here
and fell at the feet of the sage Aṟavaṇaṉ,
whose wisdom is free from flaws and who has renounced vice.
Suffering very much and confused at heart,
[I] told [Aṟavaṇaṉ] about the the cruel tragedy suffered by [my] husband/for the sake of [my] husband.
'Those who are born experience great grief;
those who are not born experience very great bliss.
the latter is experienced by those who detach. May [you] know [this truth]!'
Demonstrating [to me] the peace [to be obtained by practising] the five types of sīla,
the (mentally) strong man graced [me] by concluding:

gramGrp.gram: verbal root as peyareccam imperfective aspect
verbal root as peyareccam imperfective aspect

entry.def: dance performed in the presence of a king
dance performed before the public [opposite of vēttiyal]

entry.def: become expert in a branch of knowledge
